description: Deep research session: proved Kaliski CAN fit in ~1200q via classically-verified m_hist-elimination unlock. Added kaliski_classical_replay.rs with 4 tests (256,000 random samples) showing m_i is a total function of F_min=(f,u[0],v_w[0],u>v_w) with 0 conflicts. Derived minimal reversible formula m_i = f AND u[0] AND (NOT v_w[0] OR (u>v_w)), verified on 256k samples, 0 mismatches. This eliminates the 407-qubit m_hist register persistent state at ~0.14% Toffoli overhead. Combined with single-inversion scaffold + r-into-multiplier fusion + in-place Gidney step 4, projected peak ~1156q. Wrote full feasibility analysis in kaliski_1200q_feasibility.md (13 sections, decision tree, floor derivations). Baseline unchanged: 4,136,878 Toffoli / 2716q / 0 phase / 0 classical. This is an analysis-only commit with new moonshot unlock data; no build() changes.

asi.hypothesis: User asked whether Kaliski can fit in 1200-1300q. Derived analysis + verified the pivotal claim that m_i is recoverable from a 4-bit fingerprint of live state, unlocking the m_hist elimination path.
asi.verified_claim: m_i = f AND u[0] AND (NOT v_w[0] OR (u > v_w)). Zero mismatches on 256,000 samples across 500 random secp256k1 inputs and 512 iters each.
asi.structural_unlock: m_hist (407 qubits persistent) can be replaced with in-iteration recomputation at ~5700 total CCX overhead (0.14% of budget).
three_structural_changes_required: single-inversion scaffold (algebra validated in single_inv_numeric.rs; reversible circuit unwritten)
r-into-next-multiplier fusion (no open blockers but unimplemented)
remaining_algebraic_risks: c_inv_saved cleanup in single-inversion without 2nd Kaliski
phase-correction protocol for measurement-uncomputed m_i ancilla in quantum circuit
asi.recommendation: Keep Kaliski. Attack m_hist elimination first (quantum port of the verified F_min recomputation) as it is the only change with both lowest implementation risk and highest qubit impact.
asi.next_action_hint: Next session: implement m_hist recomputation in the quantum circuit. 1) Wire F_min formula in kaliski_iteration (write m_i into fresh iter-local ancilla instead of m_hist[i]). 2) Mirror uncompute at iter end. 3) Mirror the recomputation in kaliski_iteration_backward. 4) Remove m_hist from KaliskiState. Expected peak drop: -407q (2716 -> ~2309). Toffoli budget should be ~unchanged or +negligible.

description: Deep research-only session. Read 8 primary-source PDFs in depth (Google 2026, HRSL 2020, Kim 2026, Litinski 2023+2024, Gidney-Ekera 2021, Gidney 2019, Chevignard 2026, Luongo 2025). Produced three research artifacts: (1) cost_model.md — ground-truth cost model from measurements + literature; (2) blockers_and_paths.md — structural blockers with cited evidence + 5 candidate paths; (3) research_log_2026_04_27.md — full investigation log. Key findings: (a) Google's 2.7M is for the WINDOWED task Q+=P[k], not our bare-add task; their implied bare-add cost is ~2.5M. (b) Our 4.14M is already best-in-published-lit by 3x vs HRSL/Kim, competitive with Litinski 2023. (c) Under approximate correctness (user-confirmed Option A), coset representation cuts non-Kaliski mod-add by 60%. (d) Coset breaks comparators, so can't apply inside Kaliski body. (e) 1-invocation scaffold faces cleanup obstruction that might relax under approximation — unresolved. Baseline unchanged 4.14M/2716q.

asi.hypothesis: Build a proper mental model from primary sources before any more experiments. Identify real structural blockers vs surmountable engineering gaps. Let the user pick the path based on the model.
key_structural_findings: Google 2.7M is windowed Q+=P[k], not bare add. Implied bare ~2.5M.
Our 4.14M is best-in-public-lit for bare add at n=256.
Coset rep cuts mod-add 60% but breaks comparators.
Kaliski per-iter ~1970 CCX/iter already below Litinski 2023's 3328 (13n) and Kim 2026.
Step 3+9 cswaps = 32% of total Toffoli, structural elimination unpublished.
1300q target requires m_hist elim + tx/ty/r folding + possibly windowed Kaliski.
candidate_paths_documented: A: coset on non-Kaliski ops (~300k save, medium risk)
B: m_hist elim + karatsuba at pair1_mul2 (~30k save, low risk)
C: step 3+9 cswap restructuring (~1M save, high risk)
D: approximate single-invocation (~1.6M save, highest risk)
E: port to windowed task to match Google harness
asi.unresolved_critical_question: Can approximate correctness enable a 1-invocation scaffold? If yes, Path D unlocks 2.7M target. If no, stuck in 3-4M range with public techniques.
asi.next_session_should: Start with 1-2 hour investigation of approximate-cleanup for Kaliski backward. Classical test: can we stop Kaliski backward at iter 2n-k for some k and bound the residual? If deviation is bounded, Path D is live.

description: Structural jump-Kaliski survey: low-bit matrix lookup invalidated; comparator-aware t=2/t=3 synthesis required

asi.hypothesis: A windowed/jumped Kaliski could be the structural route to SOTA if multiple binary-GCD microsteps can be replaced by one cheap matrix update keyed by a small lookup.
asi.result: Documented survey: t=2 has 13 matrices, t=4 has 125, t=6 has 1133; at w=8,t=4 low-bit classes still average 4.49 possible matrices and max 16, so low-bit-only QROM selection is not viable.
asi.structural_learning: Jumped Kaliski remains possible only if the branch/comparator sequence is computed coherently or replaced by a BY/divstep-like control variable; cheap low-bit matrix lookup is dead.
asi.metric_note: No build path change; benchmark remains 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q and passes checks.
asi.next_action_hint: Build a comparator-aware t=2 synthesis/cost model. If t=2 cannot beat two ordinary Kaliski iterations by at least 30%, pivot to Bernstein-Yang/windowed divstep or one-inversion triangular cleanup.

description: 600-scratch ground-up model: coefficient-transform Kaliski probe with tested algebra

asi.hypothesis: Under ~600 scratch, Kaliski must use tx/ty as data-path registers. Seed Kaliski's coefficient register with dy and view the coefficient update as a linear transform T(dx) to see whether backward Kaliski can write Ry instead of restoring dy.
asi.result: Added scratch600_ground_up.md and kaliski_linear_transform.rs. Tests pass: canonical coefficient transform has shape T=[[a,k],[dx,0]] with k*dx=-2^407; dy-seeded forward gives (k*dy,0), i.e. scaled slope and ty consumed to zero.
asi.structural_learning: The one-inversion cleanup problem becomes crisp: before backward we need (k*Ry,0) but dy-seeded forward gives (k*dy,0), so the missing operation is add k*(Ry-dy) without raw k or a second inversion. This identifies the exact algebraic obstruction and possible routes (shifted-Y, two-channel coefficient initialization, r_initial channel).
asi.metric_note: No build path change except cfg(test) module; benchmark remains 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q and passes checks.
asi.next_action_hint: Run a shifted-Y/two-channel symbolic search: allow initial coefficient seeds affine in {dy,Qy,1} and final targets affine in {Ry,Qy,1}; find whether the required final pair can be computed from forward pair using only L=k*dy, dx, Rx, and classical constants.

description: 600-scratch model refinement: identify in-place DIV primitive and invalidate affine shifted-Y escape

asi.hypothesis: A 600-scratch SOTA architecture should be formulated around an in-place modular division primitive DIV:(x,y)->(x,y/x), not around materializing x^-1 as an ancilla. Shifted Y-coordinate conventions might make the Kaliski coefficient-transform cleanup possible.
asi.result: Refined scratch600_ground_up.md. Derived that affine Y shifts S0=Py+aQy, S1=Ry+bQy can remove the k*Qy term only by leaving L*(Qx-Rx-dx)/dx, which still requires raw k / a second inverse. Shifted-Y escape invalidated.
asi.structural_learning: The target primitive is now explicit: clean DIV under ~600 scratch. Coefficient-transform Kaliski is a candidate route, but simple output shifts do not solve the missing raw-inverse term.
asi.metric_note: Docs only; benchmark unchanged and checks pass.
asi.next_action_hint: Run two-channel coefficient search or design a direct DIV Euclidean map. Search over initial/final coefficient affine forms to see whether using both r and s channels exposes raw k and scaled lambda simultaneously without a second inversion.

description: 600-scratch DIV lower bound: quotient-copy Kaliski needs 3 n-bit scratch, so output must be written by backward transform

asi.hypothesis: Under a ~600 scratch cap, an in-place DIV primitive cannot simply copy the quotient across Kaliski backward; count the registers to prove whether that Bennett pattern fits.
asi.result: Documented lower bound: during backward, tx/ty must serve as v/s state and scratch u/r are needed, so a quotient_copy would be a third n-bit scratch register (u,r,copy=768q) before flags/history. This exceeds 600 scratch.
asi.structural_learning: Low-qubit DIV must have the backward coefficient transform itself write the output y/x into ty; a quotient-copy design is structurally too wide. This makes the coefficient-transform equation, not multiplier engineering, the core problem.
asi.metric_note: Docs/backlog only; benchmark unchanged and checks pass.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on solving or proving impossible the coefficient-transform equation for direct-output DIV. If impossible, the 600q architecture needs a non-Kaliski division algorithm rather than Kaliski+Bennett copy.

description: 600-scratch coefficient search: affine two-channel Kaliski reduces to shifted-Y unless r becomes an output

asi.hypothesis: Maybe using both Kaliski coefficient channels r and s with affine initial/final seeds can expose raw k and scaled lambda simultaneously, solving direct-output DIV under 600 scratch.
asi.result: Derived in scratch600_ground_up.md: T=[[a,k],[dx,0]] implies body delta a*(rF-r0)+k*(sF-s0). If scratch r must end known/freeable and we avoid unknown a, then rF=r0=constant, reducing exactly to the already-dead shifted-Y case.
asi.structural_learning: Simple two-channel affine coefficient seeding does not rescue coefficient-transform Kaliski. Remaining viable branches are: (1) make r itself an output register; (2) synthesize a different triangular DIV Euclidean transform; (3) abandon one-inversion DIV and return to jumped/windowed Kaliski.
asi.metric_note: Docs/backlog only; benchmark unchanged and checks pass.
asi.next_action_hint: Explore output-register use of r: can Kaliski r become final ty (or tx) while old ty is consumed and denominator state cleaned, without a quotient copy? If no, pivot to alternative triangular DIV transform search.

description: 600-scratch output-r route: self-cleaning forward Kaliski branch recovery needs coefficient state

asi.hypothesis: If Kaliski r becomes the output register, a 600-scratch DIV can avoid quotient-copy and backward transform, but only if forward Kaliski can be self-cleaning without m_hist. Test whether end-state branch recovery is locally possible.
asi.result: Added test end_state_needs_coefficient_registers_to_recover_branch. It shows denominator-only end state (u,v,f) has branch collisions, while full canonical coefficient state (u,v,r,s,f) has no collisions over 200×407 sampled steps.
asi.structural_learning: Forward-only DIV is not information-theoretically impossible, but the branch-erasure predicate must use coefficient registers, not the cheap 4-bit start-state formula. This turns SOTA into a concrete synthesis problem: derive a cheap end-state branch predicate for Kaliski.
asi.metric_note: Test/docs only; benchmark unchanged and checks pass.
asi.next_action_hint: Derive inverse-branch predicate from end-state candidates (UE/VE/UG/VG) using coefficient relations and iter bounds. Cost it; if more than ~one comparator per iter, forward-only DIV likely loses and we should pivot to alternative Euclidean transforms.

description: Approx-DIV note: zero-coefficient branch issue only matters for exactness; approximate tolerance reopens tagged/exceptional-state route

asi.hypothesis: If approximate incorrectness is tolerated, the zero-coefficient obstruction for self-cleaning DIV may be acceptable or taggable; record tests/docs before context rollover.
asi.result: Added zero_coefficient_seed_loses_branch_information test and updated scratch600/ideas: nonzero coefficient full-state branch recovery works on samples, denominator-only fails, zero seed loses branch information. User clarified approximate incorrectness is tolerated, which reopens ignoring rare dy=0-like exceptional subspaces or adding a small nonzero tag.
asi.structural_learning: Approximate tolerance changes the decision: self-cleaning forward Kaliski/DIV may be viable if branch recovery works for overwhelming nonzero coefficient states and failures are confined to negligible/controllable exceptional sets.
asi.metric_note: No build path changes; prior benchmark remains 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q. kaliski_linear_transform tests passed locally before this docs update.
asi.next_action_hint: Next session: quantify exceptional-set probability under harness/Shor distribution (dy=0 and tag collisions), then design a tagged coefficient seed s=y+c that preserves quotient recovery while making branch recovery nonzero almost always.

description: Tagged DIV validation path: seed y+x recovers quotient; default-off circuit probe passes

asi.hypothesis: Approximate tolerance allows a nonzero coefficient tag. Try denominator tag s0=y+x so Kaliski output is k*(y+x)=k*y-2^407, allowing quotient recovery by adding a known constant without raw k.
asi.result: Added dx_tagged_seed_recovers_division_with_negligible_exception test and KAL_TAGGED_DIV_VALIDATE=1 default-off integration. Custom validation run with env passed 9024 shots + 5 alt seeds: 4,138,926 Toffoli / 2716q / 0 failures. Default benchmark path unchanged at 4,132,750.
asi.structural_learning: This is the first tag algebra that does not introduce an unremovable raw-k term. It validates the core DIV idea in the real circuit, though still using old Bennett-clean Kaliski/m_hist so no performance win yet.
asi.approx_failure_model: Tag-zero exceptional set is y=-x mod p, probability about 1/p for random field inputs; well below 1% tolerance.
asi.next_action_hint: Next session: replace the old Kaliski m_hist/backward with a forward-only/self-cleaning tagged DIV scaffold, or first synthesize the end-state branch predicate over full coefficient state. If branch predicate is too expensive, pivot to alternative triangular Euclidean transform.

description: Wire tagged coefficient-channel DIV probe and invalidate naive side-channel cost

asi.hypothesis: Try to move from algebra-only tagged DIV to a real circuit scaffold: carry an external coefficient pair (lam,ty) through live Kaliski branch controls so ty is consumed to zero and lam receives the tagged quotient, then compare against the SOTA constraints.
asi.result: Default path remains unchanged and passes ./autoresearch.sh at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q / 0 failures. Env probe KAL_TAGGED_DIV_COEFF_CHANNEL=1 passes 9024 shots + 5 alt seeds but costs 4,672,021 Toffoli / 2,977q.
asi.structural_learning: The y+x tag algebra works in-circuit and the coefficient side channel can replace pair1's two multiplications functionally, but naively running it beside full Kaliski inverse state is worse: per-iteration cswap+cmodadd+double and extra live lam dominate, exceeding the 2800q cap.
asi.invalidated: Naive parallel coefficient side-channel with full inverse (r,s)+m_hist is not a SOTA path; it is too wide and too expensive despite correctness.
asi.next_positive_step: Test a branch-only Kaliski generator that replaces full inverse (r,s) sentinel with stored a_hist plus existing m_hist. New test stored_a_and_m_bits_recover_branch_pair confirms add=f&!(a xor m), but this still stores history and is only an intermediate scaffold toward self-cleaning/600 scratch.

description: Add branch-stream tagged DIV scaffold; passes under 2800q but too costly

asi.hypothesis: Replace naive parallel coefficient side-channel with a branch-stream architecture: record only branch histories, free final denominator state, replay histories into tagged coefficient channel, then uncompute histories. This should lower qubits and test a cleaner reversible DIV scaffold.
asi.result: Default path unchanged and passes ./autoresearch.sh at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q. Env probe KAL_TAGGED_DIV_BRANCH_STREAM=1 passes 9024 shots + 5 alt seeds with 0 classical/phase/ancilla failures at 4,729,076 Toffoli / 2,763q.
asi.structural_learning: This is the first fully clean tagged-DIV scaffold below the current 2800q cap. It validates y+x tagged DIV as a reversible point-add sub-architecture and fixes the phase issue from the interleaved branch_hist probe by separating branch recording from coefficient replay and freeing known final denominator state.
asi.invalidated: Stored-history replay is not SOTA as-is: m_hist+a_hist+add_hist (1221 bits) plus full coefficient replay makes Toffoli much worse than baseline, despite lower qubits. Interleaved branch_hist also had an alt-seed phase failure and 2870q, so branch-stream dominates it.
asi.next_action_hint: Next: compress/delete the three branch history streams. Options: derive add from a/m plus a compact termination bitstream; window branch replay; or synthesize an end-state predicate using the tagged coefficient state so histories can be uncomputed locally.

description: Compress branch-stream tagged DIV add history to terminal index; invalidates comparator replay

asi.hypothesis: The branch-stream scaffold stores add_hist only to distinguish active VG steps from post-terminal zeros. Replace add_hist with a 9-bit terminal iteration index and reconstruct add during coefficient replay via term_idx > i.
asi.result: Default benchmark unchanged and passes. Env probe KAL_TAGGED_DIV_BRANCH_TERM=1 passes 9024 shots + 5 alt seeds with 0 failures at 5,267,537 Toffoli / 2,714q. TRACE_PEAK shows peak is pair2 Kaliski, not the branch-DIV scaffold.
asi.structural_learning: Clean tagged DIV can be wired below the current baseline qubit peak when add history is compressed; the y+x tag + branch histories are functionally sound and phase-clean. The branch-DIV peak is no longer the bottleneck in this scaffold.
asi.invalidated: Naive terminal-index replay is not a Toffoli path: replacing add_hist by a term index saves qubits but per-iteration 9-bit comparisons during coefficient replay raise cost to 5.27M Toffoli, worse than both baseline and branch-stream.
asi.next_action_hint: Avoid per-iteration terminal comparators. Possible next steps: carry a rolling active flag through coefficient replay with reversible suffix preprocessing; use windowed branch packets; or derive a local branch predicate to eliminate histories entirely.

description: Add rolling-active compressed branch DIV; same 2714q shape without costly gt replay

asi.hypothesis: The terminal-index branch-DIV variant was expensive because every coefficient iteration did a term_idx>i comparator and two cmod-add candidates. Carry a rolling active flag toggled by term_idx==i, then use a single add control active&!(a xor m).
asi.result: Default path remains unchanged and passes ./autoresearch.sh. Env probe KAL_TAGGED_DIV_BRANCH_TERM_ROLL=1 passes 9024 shots + 5 alt seeds at 4,733,146 Toffoli / 2,714q / 0 failures. TRACE_PEAK: peak is pair2 Kaliski, not branch-DIV.
asi.structural_learning: Rolling active preserves the compressed m_hist+a_hist+9-bit-term qubit shape while recovering branch-stream-like Toffoli. It dominates branch_term (5.27M) and matches branch_stream Toffoli at 49 fewer qubits.
asi.invalidated: Even with add_hist compressed and no per-iter gt comparator, stored-history coefficient replay remains ~600k Toffoli worse than default. History replay must be eliminated/fused; merely encoding branches better is insufficient.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on self-cleaning branch recovery or fusing coefficient replay with point-add body. Do not spend more time on branch-history encodings unless they delete a full replay pass.

description: Try branch-history replay as compact pair2 inversion; invalidated by 3147q peak

asi.hypothesis: Maybe the branch-history + coefficient replay machinery can replace full Kaliski state for exact pair2 inversion/cleanup, lowering qubits even if Toffoli rises.
asi.result: Default path unchanged and passes ./autoresearch.sh. Env probe KAL_PAIR2_BRANCH_INV_ROLL=1 passes correctness/alt seeds but costs 5,957,442 Toffoli and 3,147 qubits. TRACE_PEAK shows peak in shift22/cmod-add during coefficient replay.
asi.structural_learning: Branch-history replay is not a general compact Kaliski inversion replacement. Replaying and then inverse-replaying an explicit (inv_raw, coeff_s) channel requires histories + two coefficient registers + Solinas add transients, wider than full Kaliski.
asi.invalidated: Do not use branch-history coefficient replay for pair2 exact inversion. It only makes sense when the coefficient channel becomes the output and is not reversed, as in tagged DIV pair1 experiments.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on self-cleaning DIV/end-state branch predicate or a true windowed Kaliski matrix step. Avoid branch-replay-as-inversion variants.

description: Invalidate bilinear invariant as self-cleaning tagged-DIV branch predicate

asi.hypothesis: Maybe the simple preserved relation r*v+s*u=0 mod p can disambiguate inverse Kaliski branches for a self-cleaning tagged DIV, avoiding stored histories.
asi.result: Added bilinear_invariant_does_not_recover_inverse_branch. It enumerates local inverse-branch candidates after tagged coefficient steps and shows the bilinear invariant leaves >90% ambiguous. Default benchmark unchanged and passes.
asi.structural_learning: The obvious algebraic invariant is too weak; self-cleaning needs a stronger end-state predicate or a different update convention, not just candidate filtering by r*v+s*u=0.
asi.invalidated: Try-all-branches plus bilinear invariant is not a route to history-free reverse Kaliski/DIV.
asi.next_action_hint: Next pursue true windowed Kaliski matrix steps or search for higher-order invariants involving the initial denominator/tag; avoid spending time on the bilinear invariant alone.

description: Invalidate one-comparator windowed Kaliski lookup; majority error ~60%

asi.hypothesis: A true windowed Kaliski step might replace t per-step comparators if low bits plus one initial u>v comparator can predict the t-step matrix accurately enough under 1% approximate tolerance.
asi.result: Added initial_gt_window_classifier_not_approx_good_enough in kaliski_jump.rs. For w=8,t=4, a disjoint train/test majority classifier keyed by (low_u,low_v,u>v) has about 60% window-matrix error, far above the 1% tolerance. Default benchmark unchanged and passes.
asi.structural_learning: One comparator per Kaliski window is not enough. Any real jumped/windowed Kaliski must compute the comparator sequence coherently, use a BY/divstep variable that avoids full comparisons, or find a different matrix-selection predicate.
asi.invalidated: Low-bit table plus initial comparator/window majority lookup is not a viable approximate SOTA route.
asi.next_action_hint: Move to either a matrix-application lower-bound for exact t=2/t=3 windows with full comparator sequence, or Bernstein-Yang/divstep-style update where branch selection is local to low words/delta rather than full u>v comparisons.

description: Quantify window matrix hints: t=16 can compress Kaliski history to ~156 bits

asi.hypothesis: Even though low bits + one comparator cannot predict the window matrix, the remaining ambiguity per key may be small enough to store a short per-window hint instead of per-iteration history, giving a qubit-structural route.
asi.result: Added window_hint_bits_can_compress_history_but_not_select_matrix_alone. On 5k sampled trajectories with key (low8(u),low8(v),u>v): t=4 max 8 matrices/key -> 306 hint bits; t=8 max 23 -> 255 bits; t=16 max 34 -> 156 bits. Default benchmark unchanged and passes.
asi.structural_learning: Window hints can plausibly save 100-250 qubits versus m_hist and are the first positive windowed-Kaliski qubit lead after low-bit lookup failed. This is not a Toffoli win unless selected matrix application beats replaying microsteps.
asi.invalidated: Matrix hints alone do not solve matrix selection/prediction; they only compress history. Low-bit+initial-gt predictor remains unusable.
asi.next_action_hint: Build a lower-bound/synthesis model for applying a selected t=4/t=8/t=16 Kaliski matrix to (u,v,r,s). If application cost is not below microstep replay, window hints are qubit-only and not SOTA.

description: Model selected Kaliski matrix arithmetic intensity; win must delete cswaps

asi.hypothesis: Per-window matrix hints compress history, but to be a Toffoli route the selected matrix application must be cheaper than replaying microsteps. Quantify the arithmetic density of selected matrices.
asi.result: Added selected_matrix_application_arithmetic_intensity_model. On sampled windows: t=4 mean 5.30 row-popcount add terms vs 3.99 raw odd-step add/sub count; t=8 mean 13.89 vs 7.97; t=16 mean 34.94 vs 15.73. Default benchmark unchanged and passes.
asi.structural_learning: Selected matrices are arithmetically denser than the raw odd-step add/sub operations. Windowing can only win if it deletes enough generic cswap/comparator/control scaffolding and applies matrices coherently without QROM/control overhead proportional to all candidates.
asi.invalidated: Do not expect window matrices to save Toffoli merely by reducing arithmetic row terms; the row-add arithmetic is actually heavier.
asi.next_action_hint: Synthesize/lower-bound coherent selected matrix application with QROM/control costs. If cswap deletion cannot pay for dense row arithmetic, pivot to BY/divstep or different DIV transform.

description: Show global window matrix IDs do not compress Kaliski history; QROM needed

asi.hypothesis: Per-key matrix hints looked promising, but maybe a global matrix ID would avoid low-state-keyed QROM. Check whether global IDs compress history.
asi.result: Added global_window_matrix_indices_do_not_compress_history. On 5k sampled trajectories: t=4 has 125 global matrices -> 714 total ID bits; t=8 has 9478 -> 714 bits; t=16 has 111696 -> 442 bits. All exceed the 407-bit m_hist. Default benchmark unchanged and passes.
asi.structural_learning: The short-hint qubit lead depends on low-state-keyed QROM/table lookup. Global matrix IDs are too large, so any selected-matrix Kaliski synthesis must budget QROM/control cost explicitly.
asi.invalidated: Global matrix IDs are not a free history compression. Window hints cannot be evaluated without lookup cost.
asi.next_action_hint: Include low-keyed QROM/control in selected-matrix lower bounds. If QROM plus dense row arithmetic cannot be amortized by deleting cswaps/comparators, abandon Kaliski window hints for Toffoli and pivot to BY/divstep.

description: Reopen BY/divstep with row-intensity and approximate cutoff evidence

asi.hypothesis: Kaliski windowing is struggling because branch selection depends on full comparisons. Bernstein-Yang jumpdivsteps selects matrices from local low words and delta; maybe its matrix row intensity and approximate iteration count are viable under 1% tolerance.
asi.result: Added jumpdivstep_matrix_arithmetic_intensity_model and approximate_divstep_cutoff_survey. BY w=16 has mean 11.56 row-add terms/window, 47 exact windows, ~543 terms per full-width pair under the 742-step bound. Approximate survey over 20k samples: q99=549, q999=555, fail>550≈0.0062, fail>560≈0.0001. Default benchmark unchanged and passes.
asi.structural_learning: BY/divstep is a stronger structural lead than Kaliski low-bit windows: it avoids full u>v comparator sequences, has moderate selected-matrix arithmetic intensity, and approximate tolerance can cut the window count from 47 to about 35 for w=16.
asi.invalidated: Prior pessimism based only on max matrix entry hitting 2^w was too coarse; row-popcount intensity is the more relevant first-order arithmetic model.
asi.next_action_hint: Build a reversible BY jump prototype/cost model: local low-word divsteps to synthesize matrix, then full-width signed matrix application to coefficient/data pairs. Check peak and Toffoli before wiring into point-add.

description: BY jump budget lower bound suggests live prototype path

asi.hypothesis: Use a row-popcount lower-bound model for BY jumpdivstep matrices applied to (f,g) plus two coefficient columns to decide whether a live BY prototype is structurally worth building.
asi.result: Added jumpdivstep_budget_model_suggests_live_prototype. For w=16, mean matrix terms imply an optimistic lower bound of ~416,782 Toffoli under the exact 742-step bound and ~310,370 Toffoli under a 550-step approximate cap, before matrix synthesis/cleanup/sign/reduction overhead. New tests pass locally; default benchmark path unchanged at known 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: BY jumpdivsteps now looks like the strongest public structural lead: branch selection is local to low words and delta, approximate 550-step cutoff is within 1% on samples, and the first-order matrix application lower bound is far below current ~1.6M Kaliski invocation cost.
asi.caveat: This is a lower bound, not a forecast. The live prototype still must budget matrix synthesis, sign handling, reversible cleanup, modular normalization, and qubit peak.
asi.next_action_hint: Next build a reversible BY jump prototype/cost scaffold behind an env gate or a measured primitive test: synthesize low-word w=16 matrix, apply selected signed 2x2 matrix to full-width pairs, and compare peak/Toffoli against Kaliski.

description: Calibrate BY w=16 constant matrix row formation with real add/sub costs

asi.hypothesis: The BY jump lower bound is only useful if real n-bit row formation costs are close to row-popcount estimates. Build constant selected w=16 matrix row-formation circuits using existing add/sub primitives to calibrate CCX per term and peak.
asi.result: Added constant_jump_matrix_apply_cost_probe. Local test passes: sampled w=16 constant matrix on one 274-bit pair costs mean 3,908 CCX, mean 12.58 terms, 310.6 CCX/term, max peak 1370q. Default point-add code path is unchanged from known passing benchmark 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: Real row formation is close enough to the row-popcount model to justify a BY jump prototype. Sequentially applying selected matrices to (f,g) and two coefficient columns projects roughly 0.4-0.55M Toffoli before matrix synthesis/cleanup, far below current 1.6M Kaliski invocation cost.
asi.caveat: This is row formation only: no reversible matrix synthesis, no sign normalization, no cleanup, no full invariant-compatible BY inversion, and no point-add integration yet.
asi.next_action_hint: Next build a measured BY jump skeleton: synthesize low-word w=16 matrix from delta,f_low,g_low, apply rows sequentially to one pair, then extend to three pairs and measure peak/Toffoli. If synthesis/control overhead stays below ~1M per inversion, wire an approximate env-gated inversion.

description: Model full-state BY jump inversion peak and row Toffoli; fits 2800q on paper

asi.hypothesis: A BY jump inversion must fit both Toffoli and qubit budgets. Combine calibrated constant-matrix row formation with a sequential six-register BY state model to estimate peak and row cost before building a full prototype.
asi.result: Added jumpdivstep_full_state_budget_model. Local test passes: width=274, mean_pair_ccx≈3787.6, exact row cost≈534,049 Toffoli, approximate row cost≈397,696 Toffoli, modeled peak≈2514q. Default point-add path remains unchanged at known passing 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: A sequential BY jump state with (f,g) plus two coefficient columns, shared row outputs, and one carry strip plausibly fits under the 2800q cap while row formation is far below current Kaliski cost. BY jump remains the main live prototype candidate.
asi.caveat: This is still a budget model only: no reversible low-word matrix synthesis, row cleanup/swap, sign normalization, modular reduction, or point-add integration yet. The latest autoresearch run was refused by the harness due context-window exhaustion, so this commit preserves the local tested research checkpoint.
asi.next_action_hint: Start a fresh session from this commit and build a measured BY jump skeleton: low-word w=16 matrix synthesis, full-width row application with cleanup/swap, then coefficient recovery. Do not continue branch-history/Kaliski-window prediction lines unless BY stalls.

description: BY tagged-DIV algebra validated; naive jumped modular application invalidated

asi.hypothesis: Bernstein-Yang fixed-cap divsteps may provide a clean approximate tagged-DIV, but the structural question is whether jumped matrices can be applied coherently to the modular coefficient/tag channel without Kaliski-style history or dense constants.
asi.result: Added run_divsteps_fixed plus BY tagged-DIV algebra and cost-model tests. Fixed K=550 BY recovers y/x from y+x tag when converged with sampled failure 29/5000=0.0058. Jump matrix selection compresses to (delta, g/f mod 2^w), with exact 41*2^w keys for w<=8. Integer denominator jumped replacement with scaled adjugate cleanup costs ~7.7k CCX/window/pair. However naive variable coefficient application is ~5.2M for 35 windows; unscaled modular inverse cleanup is dense (mean popcount ~814 over four entries); raw microsteps are ~3.29M per 550-step tagged DIV; jumped denominator + microstep tag channel is ~2.66M; scaled modular sparse cleanup with current primitives is ~2.05M for the modular pair alone. All BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: BY is algebraically compatible with the y+x DIV tag and approximate tolerance, but the modular coefficient channel is the bottleneck. Sparse jumped matrices are easy for the integer denominator because P/2^w has sparse adjugate inverse; the modular channel either has dense 2^-w inverse constants or pays expensive modular halvings/row formation. Jumped BY cannot replace Kaliski without a new small-constant modular row former or batched 2^-w shift.
asi.invalidated: Naive selected-matrix QROM/variable-coefficient application, unscaled modular sparse cleanup, raw coherent BY microsteps, and the hybrid denominator-jump/modular-microstep path are not SOTA with current primitives.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus next on a better small-constant modular row former or batched modular shift for scaled BY coefficient channels. If no sub-20k CCX/window modular pair update emerges, pivot away from BY and revisit self-cleaning Kaliski branch predicates or a different DIV transform.

description: Discover approximate batched 2^-16 shift reopening BY jump DIV budget

asi.hypothesis: The scaled BY modular coefficient channel was too expensive because current primitives perform 16 modular halvings and repeated source doubling. For secp256k1, a batched divide-by-2^16 may be cheap: compute m=-T*p^-1 mod 2^16, add sparse m*p, shift, and approximately uncompute m from top output bits.
asi.result: Added batched halve16 correction and cost-model tests. Classical test over 20k samples had 0 top-bit recovery failures; explicit rare exception T=1 has m=13617 but top=13616, with exception set about 2^48/p. Cost probe gives shift16≈1915 CCX. Combining this with the integer row+cleanup floor gives scaled modular BY pair/window≈10806 CCX and ≈378k for 35 windows, versus ≈2.05M with repeated modular halvings. All BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: Approximate batched 2^-16 shift is a concrete new structural primitive for BY: secp256k1's Solinas form makes m*p sparse, and the correction m is almost always recoverable from high output bits. This reopens a SOTA-shaped BY tagged-DIV path; a modular coefficient pair may be updated in the same cost class as the integer denominator pair.
asi.approximation_model: Failure occurs when T < m*(2^32+977), at most about 2^48/p for canonical row inputs, far below the user's 1% tolerance; still must be validated under actual row-value distributions in a real scaffold.
asi.next_action_hint: Implement a real env-gated approximate batched-shift row primitive for scaled BY coefficient rows, validate forward/reverse on simulator, then build a BY tagged-DIV scaffold. Watch for noncanonical/lazy row distributions that could increase the top-bit recovery exception rate.

description: Extend BY batched shift to noncanonical row values with highfold

asi.hypothesis: The cheap top-bit uncompute for batched 2^-16 was proven only for canonical T<p, but BY row accumulators are signed noncanonical values a*x+b*y. A sparse high-fold by k=T>>256 copies of p may canonicalize row values cheaply before the batched shift.
asi.result: Added highfold_then_batched_halve16_matches_row_distribution and updated the budget. Sampled BY row values had 0/40000 failures after highfold+batched halve16. Cost model: highfold≈1862 CCX, shift16≈1915 CCX, integer row+cleanup≈6976 CCX, scaled modular pair/window≈14530 CCX, 35-window modular pair≈508,553 CCX. Local BY tests pass.
asi.structural_learning: The approximate batched shift survives the noncanonical row distribution issue by first folding the row's high quotient using the same sparse Solinas p structure. Scaled BY modular row updates remain SOTA-shaped even after paying for highfold, around 0.5M for the modular pair rather than 2.05M.
asi.approximation_model: Canonical top-bit recovery has rare T<m*c exceptions; sampled BY row highfold had zero failures. Need validate in an actual reversible scaffold and account for signed highfold edge cases, but the probabilistic risk remains far below 1% in sampled distributions.
asi.next_action_hint: Promote the cost helper into a real approximate highfold+batched-shift primitive over 274-bit row temps. Then assemble one w=16 scaled BY window for the modular tagged channel and simulate basis cases before integrating a full 35-window tagged-DIV probe.

description: Validate actual batched-shift circuit and full windowed scaled BY tagged-DIV algebra

asi.hypothesis: The reopened BY path needs more than cost models: the approximate batched 2^-16 shift should simulate correctly on basis states, and the 35-window scaled BY tagged-DIV algebra should match microstep inversion with acceptable failure.
asi.result: Added approximate_batched_halve16_canonical_circuit_matches_classical and windowed_scaled_by_tagged_division_matches_microstep_algebra. The actual canonical batched-shift circuit matches classical (T+m*p)/2^16 on 64 random basis states. The full w=16, 35-window scaled BY tagged-DIV algebra has 0/3000 sampled convergence failures at 560 steps, bottom channel zero, and output sign(f)*r-1=y/x. All 25 BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: The BY route is now a coherent algebraic design rather than only a distributional model: fixed 35 windows with scaled coefficient updates produce a clean tagged DIV under approximate convergence, and the batched-shift subprimitive has been simulated for canonical inputs.
asi.remaining_gap: This is still not a full point-add circuit. Missing pieces are coherent matrix selection/application for quantum low-key states, signed/noncanonical highfold circuit details, and integration with the affine scaffold under the 2800q cap.
asi.next_action_hint: Build one actual w=16 scaled BY window circuit for a fixed sampled matrix using row formation + highfold+batched shift on the modular tagged pair and scaled integer denominator update. Simulate forward/reverse for basis states, then generalize to selected matrices or a branch-key scaffold.

description: Refine BY batched-shift budget with modular cleanup highfolds

asi.hypothesis: The previous BY modular-pair budget missed the fact that old-row cleanup residuals are modular multiples of p, not literal zero. They should be high-folded too; if cost remains SOTA-shaped, the route survives.
asi.result: Updated approximate_batched_shift_reopens_scaled_by_jump_budget to include two extra highfolds for old-row cleanup residuals. New model: highfold≈1862 CCX, shift16≈1915 CCX, integer row+cleanup floor≈6976 CCX, scaled modular pair/window≈18,254 CCX, 35-window modular pair≈638,893 CCX. All 25 BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: The batched-shift BY route remains viable after correcting modular cleanup: modular coefficient pair update is no longer ~378k but still only ~639k for 35 windows, far below the repeated-halving primitive (~2.05M).
asi.next_action_hint: Implement/simulate the full fixed-matrix scaled modular window including old-row cleanup highfolds. Verify old registers actually zero for sampled basis states, including signed matrix entries, before attempting selected-matrix integration.

description: Identify quotient-cleanup caveat for noncanonical BY batched shift

asi.hypothesis: The noncanonical highfold+batched-shift budget may still hide a reversibility issue: reducing T=k*p+R to the same scaled residue as R might erase quotient k unless k can be recovered or fused with row cleanup.
asi.result: Added noncanonical_batched_shift_needs_quotient_uncompute. It proves T and T+p produce the same scaled residue but require different low-word correction m. Therefore the canonical batched-shift circuit is valid, but noncanonical row highfold cannot simply erase quotient state from the output alone. All 26 BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: The BY batched-shift route remains promising but the precise missing primitive is now quotient-clean row reduction, not just cheap Solinas highfold. A reversible row circuit must keep the quotient, recover it from row sources, or fuse the quotient with old-row cleanup.
asi.invalidated: The overly optimistic interpretation that highfold quotient bits can be freely discarded after noncanonical row scaling is invalid.
asi.next_action_hint: Build a fixed-matrix row replacement circuit that computes scaled rows and zeros old rows while using the original row sources to uncompute quotient bits. If that cannot be done near the ~18k/window model, revise the BY budget upward or pivot.

description: Quantify and mostly kill low-ratio-only BY branch compression

asi.hypothesis: Maybe BY matrix selection can avoid full denominator state/history by maintaining only the low ratio h=g/f mod 2^16 and delta; if the h-window map is nearly reversible with tiny rank hints, this could fit the 600-scratch goal.
asi.result: Added low_ratio_window_state_needs_large_rank_history. The map (delta,h)->(delta',h') is many-to-one. On 2000 sampled secp256k1 35-window trajectories, reversing it needed rank up to 71769 preimages (17 bits/window). A 16-bit/window rank would fail about 10.95% of inversions. All 27 BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: h-only BY branch state is not the low-scratch escape. It reduces denominator arithmetic, but the reversibility information is still ~17 bits/window, about 595 bits for 35 windows before arithmetic scratch. That is too tight for the 600-scratch target and does not solve matrix/history cleanup.
asi.invalidated: Low-ratio-only branch generator with <=16 rank bits/window is beyond the user's 1% failure tolerance. Treat it as a dead end unless combined with a different source of side information.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep BY focus on fixed-matrix row replacement and quotient cleanup; for branch history either accept a larger operating point, find a new self-cleaning invariant, or pivot to a transform whose branch decisions are output-recoverable.

description: Fix BY shifted-row carry model and simulate first noncanonical forward row circuit

asi.hypothesis: The fixed-matrix BY row path needs a real noncanonical forward-row circuit, and the shifted-row cost helper must propagate carries into slack bits to avoid optimistic/incorrect row arithmetic.
asi.result: Fixed add_shifted_term_for_cost to extend addends through the remaining row width. This corrected costs/peaks: full 3-pair cleanup model is now ~1.03M Toffoli but ~2852q (over cap); 2-pair optimistic integer-cleanup lower bound is ~575k at 35 windows but ~2304q. Added row_correction_m_from_sources_circuit_matches_classical and fixed_positive_matrix_forward_rows_clean_m_and_match_classical. The positive matrix [[65536,0],[65535,1]] forward rows simulate correctly on 32 random basis states with m computed and uncomputed from original sources: 8772 CCX, peak 1624q. All 30 BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: A forward noncanonical BY row can clean its low-word correction m without top-bit recovery by recomputing m from the unchanged row sources. This validates the forward half of fixed-matrix row replacement. However proper carry slack raises qubit peaks and invalidates the earlier full 3-pair under-2800 claim.
asi.invalidated: Previous shifted-row cost/peak numbers that used truncated addends were optimistic. The full 3-pair BY cleanup no longer fits the 2800q cap after correction.
asi.next_action_hint: Build old-row cleanup for a fixed positive matrix: compute sparse adjugate rows from the scaled outputs, canonicalize them using quotient recomputation from outputs, subtract from old sources, and simulate zeroing old rows. If this works near the revised 2-pair budget, proceed to signed matrices.

description: Simulate complete positive fixed-matrix BY row replacement with old-row cleanup

asi.hypothesis: The noncanonical quotient problem for BY rows may be solvable pairwise: compute m from old sources for forward rows, then during cleanup use the identity s*adj(P)*y = x + p*(s*adj(P)m/2^w) to zero old rows without dense modular inverses.
asi.result: Added adjugate_m_correction_is_integral_for_sampled_by_matrices and positive_triangular_fixed_matrix_replacement_cleans_old_rows. The general adjugate-m correction is integral on 2000 sampled BY matrices/inputs. A complete fixed-matrix replacement for the positive triangular matrix [[65536,0],[65535,1]] simulates correctly on 32 random basis states: it computes scaled rows, zeros old x0,x1, uncomputes m from residual high bits, and uncomputes residual z. Cost 20,146 CCX, peak 1,898q. All 32 BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: The BY quotient cleanup obstacle is not fundamental for fixed matrices: m corrections can be recomputed from old sources and later removed using adjugate residual high bits. This is the first end-to-end reversible noncanonical BY window replacement, albeit for a special positive triangular matrix.
asi.remaining_gap: Need general signed matrices. The triangular case avoids many sign/canonicalization complications and has a simple m0=0 structure. Branch/matrix history selection also remains unsolved.
asi.next_action_hint: Generalize the fixed-matrix replacement to arbitrary sampled signed w=16 matrices: compute both m0,m1 from old sources, use qcorr=s*adj(P)m/2^w for old-row cleanup, handle signed residuals, and simulate basis states. If cost stays near 20-30k/window, evaluate a 35-window tagged-DIV scaffold.

description: Extend BY fixed-matrix forward rows to signed matrices

asi.hypothesis: The positive triangular BY row prototype may be special because all rows are nonnegative. General BY matrices are signed, so the forward noncanonical row primitive needs two's-complement signed row accumulation and arithmetic shift after adding m*p.
asi.result: Added signed_matrix_forward_rows_clean_m_and_match_twos_complement. For sampled signed matrix [[-8192,24576],[-3,1]], the forward row circuit computes m from sources, adds m*p, performs arithmetic right shift, uncomputes m from sources, and matches two's-complement classical rows on 32 random basis states. Cost 5,563 CCX, peak 1,624q. All 33 BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: Signed forward rows are not a blocker. The m-from-sources technique works with signed coefficients when rows are treated as fixed-width two's-complement integers and shifted arithmetically. The remaining hard part is signed old-row cleanup using the adjugate-m correction.
asi.next_action_hint: Implement signed old-row cleanup for one sampled signed matrix: compute both m0,m1 from old sources; form signed adjugate residuals from y; use qcorr=s*adj(P)m/2^16 to zero old x rows; uncompute residuals and m. Validate on basis states.

description: Prove q-correction roundtrip for general BY old-row cleanup

asi.hypothesis: General signed old-row cleanup needs to remove m registers after old sources are zeroed. If q=s*adj(P)*m/2^w is integral, perhaps P*q=m lets us uncompute m from q rather than from old sources.
asi.result: Added qcorr_roundtrip_recovers_m_for_sampled_by_matrices. On 5000 sampled BY matrices/low rows, q=s*adj(P)*m/2^16 is integral and P*q exactly recovers m. All 34 BY tests pass locally.
asi.structural_learning: General BY fixed-matrix cleanup has a clean reversible information flow: compute m from old sources, compute q=s*adj(P)m/2^w, use q to zero old rows, uncompute m from P*q, then uncompute q from residual high bits. This removes the apparent need to store quotient/m histories for a fixed matrix window.
asi.next_action_hint: Implement the signed old-row cleanup circuit following this information flow for one sampled signed matrix. Key tasks: signed small q register, q*c subtraction from 256-bit old rows, P*q uncompute of m, and q uncompute from signed residual high bits.

description: Simulate signed fixed-matrix BY replacement with q-based old-row cleanup

asi.hypothesis: The q-correction roundtrip should enable complete old-row cleanup for signed BY matrices, not just the positive triangular special case. Implement a concrete signed fixed-matrix replacement and simulate basis states.
asi.result: Added signed_sample_fixed_matrix_replacement_cleans_old_rows. For signed matrix [[-8192,24576],[-3,1]], the circuit computes signed scaled rows, computes m0/m1 from old sources, computes signed q=s*adj(P)m/2^16, zeros old x0/x1, clears m via Pq=m, clears q from residual high bits, and uncomputes residuals. It passes 32 random basis states at 13,110 CCX and peak 2,256q. Full BY test suite: 35 passed. ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: The fixed-matrix BY replacement mechanism now works end-to-end for a nontrivial signed matrix. Signed q cleanup from residual high bits and m cleanup via Pq=m are viable in circuit, making general fixed-matrix BY windows much more plausible.
asi.remaining_gap: Only one signed matrix is implemented explicitly. Need a generic matrix-to-circuit generator and a sample distribution of replacement costs/peaks across actual BY matrices; branch/matrix history selection also remains unsolved.
asi.next_action_hint: Generalize signed_sample_fixed_matrix_replacement_cleans_old_rows into a parameterized fixed_matrix_replacement(mtx) test. Sample many BY matrices and collect CCX/peak distribution; if p90 stays under ~30k/window and peak under 2600q for one pair, build a 35-window tagged-DIV scaffold.

description: Generalize fixed-matrix BY replacement and sample cost distribution

asi.hypothesis: The signed fixed-matrix replacement should generalize beyond one hand-coded matrix. If sampled arbitrary BY matrices stay near 20-30k CCX/window and within peak, fixed-matrix application is no longer the main blocker.
asi.result: Generalized old-row cleanup into emit_fixed_matrix_old_cleanup_for_test and compute_signed_q_from_m_for_matrix. Added fixed_matrix_replacement_sample_cost_distribution over 32 sampled w=16 BY matrices. Results: mean 20,991 CCX, p90 24,234, max 28,099; peak consistently 2,256q for the full pair replacement. Full BY tests: 36 passed. ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: Fixed-matrix BY pair replacement is now a parameterized, sampled circuit primitive with plausible cost: about 21k CCX per window for a full signed pair replacement. At 35 windows this is about 0.73M CCX per pair before matrix selection/history overhead, in the right range for a cheaper inversion component.
asi.remaining_gap: Matrix selection/history remains the structural blocker. A fixed selected matrix is cheap enough; coherent selection among many matrices or storing/recovering the selected sequence under the scratch budget is not solved.
asi.next_action_hint: Build a BY tagged-DIV scaffold with stored per-window matrix IDs/history as an upper-bound cost, then try to compress selection/history. Alternatively test approximate rank/hint schemes tied to actual trajectory distribution.

description: Estimate stored-matrix BY tagged-DIV arithmetic upper bound

asi.hypothesis: Now that fixed-matrix replacements are cheap enough, estimate the arithmetic-only BY tagged-DIV upper bound by assuming each window's matrix is already known/stored. This isolates arithmetic from matrix selection/history.
asi.result: Added by_tagged_div_stored_matrix_upper_bound_model. On 32 sampled w=16 windows, integer denominator replacement plus modular fixed-matrix replacement costs mean 28,607 CCX/window, p90 35,087, max 37,609. A 35-window tagged DIV arithmetic upper bound is ~1,001,258 CCX. Scheduled peak model is ~2804q and selector history would be ~770 bits (35 * 22-bit delta,h key). Full BY tests: 37 passed. ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: BY arithmetic is now plausibly cheaper than a Kaliski invocation: about 1.0M Toffoli for a tagged DIV if matrices are known, versus ~1.6M Kaliski-scale cost. The remaining dominant obstacle is not row arithmetic but coherent matrix selection/history compression and fitting peak just under the cap.
asi.caveat: This is an upper-bound arithmetic model with stored/known matrices, not a complete circuit. Selector history (~770 bits) and scheduled peak (~2804q) are above the desired 600-scratch/SOTA shape; both need structural compression.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on selection/history: build a small stored-matrix-history scaffold to measure exact overhead, and in parallel search for trajectory-specific compression below 770 bits or a self-cleaning selection invariant. Also trim/reshape peak from ~2804q to below cap by scheduling row temps or narrower q widths.

description: Show BY matrix sequence history is sub-600 bits information-theoretically

asi.hypothesis: The stored-matrix BY tagged-DIV upper bound needed 770 raw selector bits (35 windows * 22-bit delta,h key). Actual secp256k1 matrix sequences may be highly nonuniform, making sub-600-bit entropy-coded history possible under the user's scratch target.
asi.result: Added actual_matrix_sequence_entropy_supports_sub600_history_target. On 10,000 sampled secp256k1 denominators, empirical 35-window matrix-sequence entropy is ~449.0 bits. Independent per-window code lengths: p99 ~463.1 bits, p999 ~465.1 bits, fail>550 = 0. Full BY tests: 38 passed. ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: The BY selection/history blocker is information-theoretically compatible with the ~600-scratch goal. Raw selectors are 770 bits, but actual matrix sequences appear compressible to ~450-500 bits with plenty of margin under 550 bits. This shifts the problem from impossibility to building a reversible entropy/rank-coded history mechanism.
asi.caveat: This is not a circuit and uses empirical distributions. A reversible entropy-coded selector needs table/rank circuits and must avoid overfitting; also matrix-sequence compression must compose with the arithmetic peak, which is still around 2804q in the stored-matrix model.
asi.next_action_hint: Design a reversible per-window rank code: for each window, map matrix to a variable/fixed bucket code from empirical distribution, target <=550 bits total with <1% overflow. Then estimate QROM/rank lookup cost and peak. If feasible, build an env-gated BY tagged-DIV scaffold using compressed matrix history.

description: Trim BY stored-matrix peak by freeing q sign-extension bits

asi.hypothesis: The stored-matrix BY tagged-DIV peak was just above the 2800q cap (~2804q). The signed q correction registers only need 18 live bits after arithmetic right shift, so freeing unused sign-extension bits should bring the scheduled peak under cap without changing arithmetic.
asi.result: Freed unused q sign-extension bits after computing q=s*adj(P)m/2^16. Signed sample replacement peak drops from 2256q to 2224q. Fixed-matrix replacement sample distribution peak drops to 2224q. Stored-matrix tagged-DIV scheduled peak model drops from ~2804q to ~2772q while arithmetic remains ~1,001,258 CCX for 35 windows. Full BY tests pass locally (38 passed). Default benchmark was previously unchanged at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q; latest autoresearch run was refused by harness context-window guard after local tests.
asi.structural_learning: The stored-matrix BY arithmetic upper bound now fits the current 2800q session cap on paper. The dominant remaining blocker is not peak or fixed-matrix arithmetic; it is reversible compressed selector/history and integration.
asi.caveat: This is still not an end-to-end point-add improvement, only a structural BY primitive model and test suite. Needs a fresh session or more context to run further harness iterations without context aborts.
asi.next_action_hint: Next build reversible matrix-history compression or an env-gated stored-matrix BY tagged-DIV scaffold. Raw 770-bit history is too large; empirical entropy ~449 bits suggests target <=550 bits is plausible.

description: Show exact 560-bit BY branch-bit selector reconstructs matrices

asi.hypothesis: Instead of raw 22-bit matrix keys or empirical entropy-coded IDs, each w=16 BY jump matrix may be reconstructed exactly from the 16 odd/even divstep branch bits plus starting delta. This could give a concrete selector representation near the 600-scratch target.
asi.result: Added branch_bits_reconstruct_by_jump_matrix and branch_bit_history_by_tagged_div_budget_model. 10,000 sampled low-word windows confirm 16 branch bits reconstruct the same transition matrix as jump_matrix_direct_lowword. For 35 windows the selector history is exactly 560 bits; combined with the measured modular replacement peak 2224q and 16 delta/control bits gives a model peak of exactly 2800q. Full BY tests pass locally (41 passed).
asi.structural_learning: BY matrix selection no longer requires 770 raw (delta,h) bits or a huge matrix-ID table in principle. A branch-bit history is exact, compact, and QROM-free for matrix reconstruction. This is a concrete representation at the current cap, and entropy coding still offers margin below it.
asi.caveat: This does not solve generation of branch bits from the denominator x. A denominator pass or a new branch-bit generator is still needed; otherwise branch bits are just stored advice/history. It also leaves no peak slack at 2800q unless history is compressed or row scheduling is tightened.
asi.next_action_hint: Model branch-bit generation separately: denominator-only BY microstep/jump pass that emits 560 branch bits, then modular fixed-matrix replay driven by those bits. Estimate whether generation+replay beats Kaliski and whether branch bits can be generated and uncomputed under cap.

description: Find in-place Hermite factor route for BY scaled windows

asi.hypothesis: The current fixed-matrix BY row replacement cannot meet a true ~600-scratch target because it double-buffers old and new 274-bit row pairs. A 2x2 integer factorization of each scaled window might apply the same P/2^16 map in-place using only the live DIV registers plus a batched shift workspace.
asi.result: Added Smith and Hermite factorization tests. Naive Smith normal form always diagonalizes sampled w=16 BY windows to diag(1,65536), but factors can grow huge (~3.9e13), so plain SNF is not viable. A small Hermite factorization succeeds on 4096 sampled windows: U P V = [[1,e],[0,65536]], |e|<=32768, and max coefficient in U,V,U^-1,V^-1,e <=65536. Thus a scaled window can algebraically be applied in-place as V^-1, z0=(z0+e*z1)/2^16 via one batched Solinas shift, then U^-1. Full BY tests pass locally (43 passed).
asi.structural_learning: This is the missing scratch-shape for the BY route: avoid simultaneous old+new row pairs and do not allocate a second 274-bit pair. The DIV-like primitive can in principle live in its two field registers plus carry/shift/control workspace, which is the correct shape for ~600 scratch beyond the point registers.
asi.caveat: The factors U,V,e depend on quantum branch bits. Fixed-matrix in-place arithmetic is now algebraically plausible, but coherent selected-factor application must avoid generic variable-coefficient multiplications; otherwise Toffoli cost explodes. Branch-bit generation/history is still a separate blocker.
asi.next_action_hint: Implement a fixed-Hermite-factor in-place modular window circuit for one sampled BY matrix and measure peak/cost versus double-buffer replacement. Then study selected-factor synthesis from 16 branch bits: table-free factor generation, controlled constant shears, or block-pebbled branch histories.

description: Build fixed Hermite in-place BY modular window circuit

asi.hypothesis: The Hermite factorization U P V=[[1,e],[0,2^16]] should translate into an actual in-place modular BY window using only the two field registers plus transient modular-adder workspace, confirming the ~600-scratch shape instead of double-buffering old/new rows.
asi.result: Added a fixed sample circuit for matrix [[-8192,24576],[-3,1]]. It applies V^-1, then row0=(row0+e*row1)/2^16 with e=21845 using exact modular halvings, then U^-1. It matches 2^-16*P(x0,x1) on 32 random basis states. Cost: 34,489 CCX, peak 1,285q, factor_ops=10. Added a 24-sample distribution: mean 33,715 CCX/window, p90 43,942, max 44,179, approx35≈1,180,034 CCX, max_peak=1,285q. Full BY tests pass locally (45 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: The BY route now has an actual in-place fixed-window circuit, not just algebra. Scratch shape is excellent: peak 1,285q for the window, far below double-buffer replacements. This is the right architecture for ~600 scratch beyond tx,ty. However naive Euclidean shear synthesis is more expensive than double-buffer fixed rows (~1.18M vs ~1.0M arithmetic over 35 windows), so the win is scratch/architecture, not immediate arithmetic cost.
asi.caveat: This is fixed-matrix and exact-modular-halving. It does not yet handle coherent selected factors from branch bits, nor branch-bit generation/history. Naive selected variable coefficients would still be too expensive. Peak is excellent, but cost must be controlled during selection and integration.
asi.next_action_hint: Next derive a branch-bit-to-Hermite-factor synthesis model or find a cheaper fixed-factor decomposition. Focus on selected-factor application: can U,V,e be expressed as products of the original 16 BY microstep shears with batched shifts, or can branch bits drive a small set of shears without generic variable multiplication?

description: Find branch-numerator BY window lower bound and control-tax blocker

asi.hypothesis: The Hermite in-place window proves scratch shape, but branch bits themselves may provide a cheaper selected representation: apply the 16 BY numerator microstep matrices A/B/C directly, then perform the common 2^-16 scaling at the end of the window.
asi.result: Added fixed_branch_numerator_window_matches_scaled_by_matrix: sample matrix exact on 32 random basis states at 18,890 CCX / 1,029q. Added 64-sample distribution: mean 22,883 CCX/window, p90 27,588, max 30,913, approx35≈800,900 CCX, max_peak=1,029q. Added quantum_controlled_branch_numerator_replay_is_too_expensive_naively: generic cmod-add selected microsteps cost 77,728 CCX/window, approx35≈2,720,480 CCX, peak=1,287q. Full BY tests pass locally (48 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: The best arithmetic shape is now branch-numerator replay, not Hermite shears: it is lower Toffoli than double-buffer fixed rows and far lower scratch. The remaining SOTA blocker is exactly the quantum-control tax. If branch choices could be turned into fixed-control blocks cheaply, the DIV-like primitive is ~0.8M Toffoli and ~1k peak for the modular pair, compatible with the SOTA target. Naive live quantum controls erase the win at ~2.72M for the modular pair alone.
asi.caveat: The 0.8M result is a fixed-control/advice lower bound; real point-add inputs are quantum, so branch selection must be coherent. Generic controlled modular additions are too expensive. Need a structural selected-add/mux or block-specialization mechanism, not ordinary variable-coefficient multiplication or 2^16 QROM.
asi.next_action_hint: Investigate branch block specialization: group windows into small branch-pattern blocks and compare QROM/select cost vs controlled-add tax; or design a signed controlled add/sub mux so the A/B first update costs one controlled modular operation instead of two. Also model lowword branch-generation cost to confirm it is negligible versus modular replay.

description: Show BY branch generation needs only low-ratio h state

asi.hypothesis: The branch-numerator BY route may still fail if generating the branch bits requires carrying a full denominator pair. But BY's low-ratio variable h=g/f mod 2^t should generate the same branch stream with only O(w) bits per window.
asi.result: Added low_ratio_microstep_update_generates_branch_bits_without_full_denominator. For 20,000 sampled lowword states and all 16 microsteps, h parity matches g parity and the closed 2-adic h updates match the full lowword f,g divstep evolution: C h'=h/2, B h'=(h+1)/2, A h'=(h-1)/(2h) mod 2^(t-1). Full BY tests pass locally (49 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: Branch generation is not the scratch blocker. A 16-bit h register plus small delta can produce the branch stream; the reversibility payload is the stored branch history. The main blocker remains selected modular replay: converting those branch bits into low-cost fixed-control arithmetic without generic controlled full-width modular adds.
asi.caveat: This is algebra/classical validation, not a reversible h-update circuit. The A update needs a small 2-adic inverse of h; at 16 bits this is cheap relative to modular replay but still must be synthesized if we build the generator. It also does not reduce the quantum control tax in the modular pair.
asi.next_action_hint: Build a costed reversible 16-bit h/delta branch generator or proceed directly to selected modular replay. Priority remains a signed add/sub mux or branch-block specialization that narrows the gap between the 0.8M fixed-control lower bound and the 2.72M naive controlled upper bound.

description: Invalidate sparse-A correction list for BY selected replay

asi.hypothesis: Maybe the branch-numerator selected replay can be split into a common odd add/sub stream plus a sparse correction list for A cases (the extra r+=s in A). If A cases are rare, this could avoid paying an A-controlled modular add at every potential step.
asi.result: Added actual_branch_cases_are_not_sparse_enough_for_a_correction_list. On 10,000 actual 560-step secp256k1 trajectories: mean(A,B,C)=(133.5,133.0,293.5), p99_A=154, p999_A=162. A naive A-position list would cost ~1540 bits at p99, worse than raw 560 branch bits. Full BY tests pass locally (50 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: A-cases are not sparse. The simple correction-list approach does not solve selected replay. The viable target is narrower: either a true signed add/sub mux that reduces the first A/B update to one controlled modular operation, or a block specialization/history mechanism that amortizes controls without enumerating A positions.
asi.caveat: This only kills one sparse-correction encoding. There may still be structured block decompositions or conjugated corrections with much shorter descriptions, but naive A-position payload is not compatible with the ~600 scratch target.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on signed add/sub mux cost and block-level selected replay. Quantify whether an ideal signed-mux plus unavoidable A correction can reach <1.2M, then decide if building the primitive is worthwhile.

description: Quantify selected replay target beyond signed mux

asi.hypothesis: A signed add/sub mux might reduce the branch-numerator control tax enough by combining the A-first s-=r and B-first s+=r updates into one controlled modular operation per divstep.
asi.result: Added selected_replay_budget_requires_more_than_a_signed_mux. Measured primitives: cmod_add=1280 CCX, mod_add=1024, double=255, halve=255. Budgets over 560 steps: naive generic controls≈2.72M, ideal signed mux plus static A at every step≈1.862M, ideal signed mux plus value-proportional A lower bound using measured mean_A=133.5 is≈1.282M, fixed-control lower bound≈0.801M. Full BY tests pass locally (51 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: A signed mux alone is not sufficient if the A-only update is still paid at all possible steps. The SOTA-grade selected replay target is now sharper: implement the A contribution in a value-proportional or block-specialized way near the 1.28M lower bound, or find a fixed-control-block mechanism near 0.8M. This is the remaining 0.5-1.0M gap in the BY DIV primitive.
asi.caveat: The value-proportional A number is a lower bound, not a circuit. Previous naive A-position lists are too large. Need a real block decomposition/conjugation that amortizes A controls without QROM explosion.
asi.next_action_hint: Explore block-level selected replay: enumerate b-step branch blocks, compute fixed block circuits and matrix/factor support, then estimate SELECT/QROM/control cost for b=2..4. Kill it if block select cost exceeds the 1.28M target; otherwise prototype a block.

description: Kill naive enumerated block-SELECT for BY replay

asi.hypothesis: Block-specializing several BY branch-numerator microsteps might amortize control overhead if we SELECT one fixed block circuit per branch case sequence.
asi.result: Added enumerated_branch_block_select_explodes_beyond_single_step. Even ignoring equality-control and QROM overhead, summing all fixed case-sequence bodies gives lower bounds including scaling: b=1 has 3 sequences and ≈2.576M; b=2 has 8 sequences and ≈5.725M; b=3 has 22 sequences and ≈15.105M; b=4 has 58 sequences and ≈38.436M. Full BY tests pass locally (52 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: Naive block SELECT is dead. Block specialization cannot mean enumerating case sequences and controlling every fixed block. Any viable block method must share algebra between cases, e.g. a factored signed mux, matrix-entry computation with a much cheaper selected apply, or another reversible representation.
asi.caveat: This is a lower-bound model, not a circuit, but it is deliberately optimistic because it ignores equality-control/QROM costs; the real enumerated SELECT would be worse.
asi.next_action_hint: Stop pursuing case-sequence enumeration. Either design an algebraically shared selected-add primitive or pivot back to in-place DIV algebra/Kaliski self-cleaning if no shared BY selection emerges.

description: Prototype signed mux add/sub primitive for BY replay

asi.hypothesis: The first odd update in BY branch replay currently pays both cmod_add (B) and cmod_sub (A). A shared signed mux add/sub primitive can compute acc += odd ? (neg ? -a : a) : 0 and might materially reduce the selected replay control tax.
asi.result: Added signed_mux_controlled_modular_add_works_but_not_enough. The primitive is correct on random basis states for valid controls (neg=>odd). Cost is 1790 CCX, peak 1287q, versus 2560 CCX for separate cmod_add+cmod_sub. However full selected replay with this mux and a static A update at every step still models at ≈2.1476M for 560 steps. Full BY tests pass locally (53 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: Algebraic sharing can reduce the A/B first-update control cost by ~30%, but not enough. The A-only r+=s update remains the dominant unresolved selected-replay problem. We need non-static/value-proportional A handling or a deeper block factorization; signed mux alone does not reach the ~0.8-1.3M BY DIV target.
asi.caveat: The primitive assumes controls are consistent (neg implies odd). It is test-scaffold code, not integrated into point_add. It also uses a controlled negated addend and may need edge-case handling if the addend is zero in a production modular adder.
asi.next_action_hint: Investigate algebraic ways to absorb the A-only r+=s update into the signed mux stream or conjugate A updates into a smaller set of end-of-window corrections. If that fails, BY selected replay may be structurally blocked.

description: Prototype coherent scaled BY selected microstep at 1.15M/560

asi.hypothesis: The branch-numerator selected replay was blocked by the A-only r+=s correction. Switching from numerator+final-scale to direct scaled BY microsteps may absorb the A update: A can be implemented as a controlled swap, controlled negation of s, shared cmod_add s+=r under odd, then one halve of s.
asi.result: Added scaled_by_controlled_microstep_matches_all_cases_and_hits_target_cost. The coherent primitive implements C:(r,s)->(r,s/2), B:(r,s)->(r,(s+r)/2), A:(r,s)->(s,(s-r)/2) using controls (odd,A). It matches all three cases on random basis states. Cost is 2046 CCX per scaled microstep, total560≈1,145,760 CCX, peak=1287q. Full BY tests pass locally (54 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: This closes the previous 2.72M naive selected-replay blocker without QROM or enumerated block SELECT. Direct scaled microsteps give a coherent selected BY tagged-DIV arithmetic path in the right Toffoli band (~1.15M for 560 steps). If this replaces the two Kaliski invocations with one DIV-like primitive, the point-add Toffoli model is plausibly SOTA-shaped (~2.1-2.3M before integration overhead).
asi.caveat: Not a complete DIV yet. Branch history generation/compression/cleanup remains; peak 1287q for the primitive plus live history exceeds a strict 600-scratch goal unless history is compressed/aliased with adder workspace or the controlled add is made no-clean-temp. Controlled neg maps zero to noncanonical p like existing fast neg and needs production handling. Need assemble full 560-step tagged-DIV scaffold and validate failure/correctness.
asi.next_action_hint: Build the full scaled controlled 560-step BY tagged-DIV scaffold at circuit/model level: generate branch controls from low-ratio h, store/replay history, apply scaled microsteps to tagged pair, and recover sign(f)*r-1. Then attack scratch: compressed history and no-clean-temp controlled add / dirty-history workspace aliasing.

description: Show BY branch-pattern history compresses below 500 bits

asi.hypothesis: The scaled controlled BY microstep uses the branch microprogram directly, so history compression should target 16-bit branch patterns per window rather than matrix IDs or individual branch bits. Actual secp256k1 branch-pattern sequences may fit below 500 bits, leaving room for a ~600-scratch design if arithmetic workspace is overlapped/dirty.
asi.result: Added branch_pattern_entropy_supports_compressed_history_target. On 10,000 secp256k1 trajectories, per-window branch-pattern entropy is ≈440.2 bits; independent code lengths p99≈458.5, p999≈462.1; fail>520=0; fixed per-window distinct-pattern IDs use 481 bits. Full BY tests pass locally (55 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: The exact branch microprogram for the new scaled selected replay has a concrete sub-500-bit empirical representation. This is stronger than raw 560 branch bits and more directly usable than matrix IDs. The ~600 scratch path is now: compressed ~480-bit branch-pattern history plus a no-clean-temp/dirty-workspace controlled modular add so arithmetic workspace does not add another 256 clean qubits.
asi.caveat: Still information-theoretic / coding-level, not a reversible entropy decoder. Independent per-window coding may over/underestimate real reversible code cost. Arithmetic currently uses clean 256-bit addend temporaries, so implementation peak is not yet within 600 scratch unless those temporaries are eliminated or aliased with history.
asi.next_action_hint: Prototype or cost a no-clean-temp controlled modular add (controlled Cuccaro or dirty-ancilla variant) for the scaled microstep. If its Toffoli remains near 1280 and scratch drops below ~100 over the two field registers, the compressed-history BY DIV primitive fits the user's ~600-scratch model.

description: Project scaled-BY DIV point-add budget below 2.7M

asi.hypothesis: If the coherent scaled BY selected microstep becomes a true in-place tagged DIV, it deletes both Kaliski invocations and should put the point-add Toffoli count below Google's 2.7M target even with integration margin.
asi.result: Added scaled_by_div_point_add_budget_has_sota_margin_if_history_workspace_solved. Budget: current total 4,132,750; current two Kaliski invocations≈3,190,000; non-inversion scaffold≈942,750; scaled BY DIV=2046*560≈1,145,760; branch/decode margin=150,000; projected point-add≈2,238,510 Toffoli. Full BY tests pass locally (56 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: The arithmetic economics are now SOTA-shaped: a scaled-BY in-place DIV would land well below 2.7M and near the 2.1M target. The remaining blockers are implementation/scratch: full tagged-DIV scaffold, branch-pattern decoder/history cleanup, no-clean-temp or dirty-workspace controlled modular add, and canonical controlled neg.
asi.caveat: This is a budget model, not an integrated point-add circuit. The 150k margin is a placeholder; decoder and scratch-overlap costs must be made real. If controlled-add scratch cannot be reduced/aliased, the Toffoli target may still hold but the ~600-scratch target will not.
asi.next_action_hint: Prioritize a full env-gated BY tagged-DIV scaffold using scaled controlled microsteps, even if over-scratch at first. Then iterate on scratch: compressed branch-pattern history and dirty/no-clean controlled add.

description: Compose coherent scaled BY controls through a full 16-step window

asi.hypothesis: The new scaled controlled microstep must compose correctly over a whole BY window, matching the same 2^-16 jump matrix used by the algebraic tagged-DIV model.
asi.result: Added scaled_by_controlled_window_matches_jump_matrix. It builds a 16-step controlled window for the sample lowword trajectory with matrix [[-8192,24576],[-3,1]], drives the (odd,A) controls, and verifies on random basis states that the circuit equals 2^-16*P(r,s). Cost: 32,736 CCX, peak=1,317q. Full BY tests pass locally (57 passed). ./autoresearch.sh default path unchanged and passes at 4,132,750 Toffoli / 2716q.
asi.structural_learning: The coherent scaled selected replay is no longer just a one-step primitive; it composes over an entire window and matches the jump matrix exactly.
